attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old man presents to the dialysis unit for his regular haemodialysis session. He has end-stage kidney disease secondary to diabetic nephropathy and has been receiving haemodialysis for three years via a left brachiocephalic arteriovenous fistula. His other medical conditions include type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and ischaemic heart disease. His medications include sitagliptin, gliclazide, insulin, amlodipine, carvedilol and weekly darbepoetin.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The dialysis nurse notes absence of thrill and bruit over his arteriovenous fistula. The patient reports the fistula was working normally at his last dialysis session two days ago.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Insert temporary dialysis catheter and continue dialysis
Attempt cannulation of the fistula at a different site
Commence immediate intravenous antibiotics to cover for infection
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with an acute loss of thrill in his arteriovenous fistula, which strongly suggests acute fistula thrombosis. The absence of both thrill and bruit indicates complete occlusion of the fistula, which is a medical emergency requiring immediate intervention to salvage the access. If the fistula is unable to be salvaged, the patient may require a line insertion +/- creation of a new fistula.

Transfer to the emergency department (for urgent vascular team review) is the most appropriate action. Early intervention (ASAP) significantly improves the chances of successful fistula salvage. The patient will require urgent vascular surgical review and imaging (typically fistulogram) to determine the cause and extent of thrombosis. This patient may also require a thrombectomy.

Insert temporary dialysis catheter and continue dialysis addresses the immediate need for dialysis but delays definitive management of the thrombosed fistula. While a temporary catheter may eventually be needed, the priority is urgent fistula assessment to salvage native access.

Arrange outpatient vascular surgery review would result in unacceptable delays. The window of opportunity for successful thrombectomy is limited, and delays reduce the likelihood of salvaging the fistula.

Attempt cannulation at a different site would be inappropriate and potentially harmful. Without a thrill, the fistula is not suitable for cannulation, and attempts could cause damage or complications.

Commence immediate intravenous antibiotics to cover for infection is incorrect as there is no suggestion of infection in the stem.

Arteriovenous fistula thrombosis is a known complication of haemodialysis access, often related to underlying stenosis. Risk factors include previous episodes of thrombosis, hypotension, and hypercoagulable states. Successful salvage requires prompt recognition and immediate referral for assessment and treatment.
learningPoints: Loss of thrill in an arteriovenous fistula requires urgent assessment as it may indicate acute thrombosis.
Immediate hospital transfer is required to maximise chances of fistula salvage.
references: Quencer KB, Oklu R. Hemodialysis access thrombosis. Cardiovascular Diagnosis and Therapy. 2017 Dec 1;7(Suppl 3):S299–308. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5778528/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
